subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand historical aspects of the uniqueness and commonality of individuals and groups, including Maine Native Americans, by explaining how individuals and families share both common and unique aspects of culture, values, and beliefs through stories, traditions, religion, celebrations, or the arts.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and families share both special and common parts of their culture through stories, traditions, celebrations, and art.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of history as well as the key foundation of ideas by applying terms such as "before" and "after" in sequencing events.
description_plain: Your child can use words like 'before' and 'after' to put events in order.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of history by describing history as stories of the past and identifying questions related to social studies.
description_plain: Your child knows that history is made up of stories about the past and can ask questions about people and events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes using primary and secondary sources from history to develop historical perspective and understand issues of continuity and change in the community, Maine, the United States, and world.
description_plain: Your child can use old and new sources to understand history and how things have changed or stayed the same in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the influence of geography on individuals and their immediate surroundings by identifying the impacts of geographic features on individuals and families.
description_plain: Your child can explain how land and places affect the people and families who live there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature and basic ideas of geography by identifying questions about their world and explaining that geography is the study of the Earth's surface and peoples.
description_plain: Your child knows that geography is the study of the Earth and people, and can ask questions about the world around them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes from geography to understand issues involving people, places, and environments in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child can use geography ideas to understand how people, places, and environments connect in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the influence of economics on individuals and groups in the United States and the World, including Maine Native Americans, by identifying how individuals, families, and communities are part of an economy.
description_plain: Your child knows that individuals, families, and communities are part of an economy and that this affects their lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of economics as well as key foundational ideas by describing how people make choices to meet their needs and wants.
description_plain: Your child understands that people make choices to get what they need and want.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of personal finance as well as key foundational ideas by describing how money has value and can be traded for goods and services.
description_plain: Your child knows that money has value and can be used to buy things or services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw from concepts and processes in personal finance to understand issues of money management, saving, investing, credit, and debt; students draw from concepts and processes in economics to understand issues of production, distribution, consumption in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child understands how to manage money, save, invest, use credit, and handle debt, and also how goods and services are made and shared in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand civic aspects of classroom traditions and decisions by identifying and comparing diverse interests and opinions related to classroom traditions and decisions.
description_plain: Your child can notice and compare different opinions and interests about classroom traditions and decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the concepts of rights, duties, responsibilities, and participation by describing classroom rights, duties, and responsibilities including how students participate in some classroom decisions and are obliged to follow classroom rules.
description_plain: Your child knows about classroom rights, duties, and responsibilities and how they help make decisions and follow rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the concepts of rights, duties, responsibilities, and participation by explaining the purpose of school/classroom rules and local laws encountered in daily experiences to promote the common good and the peaceful resolution of conflict.
description_plain: Your child understands that rules and laws help keep peace and fairness in the classroom and community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand key ideas and processes that characterize democratic government in the community and the United States by recognizing symbols, monuments, celebrations, and leaders of local government.
description_plain: Your child can recognize important symbols, celebrations, and leaders in local government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand key ideas and processes that characterize democratic government in the community and the United States by identifying community workers and volunteers and the roles they play in promoting the common good.
description_plain: Your child knows about community helpers and volunteers and how they help everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts from civics and government to understand political systems, power, authority, governance, civic ideals and practices, and the role of citizens in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child understands how governments work, the role of citizens, and how power and rules affect communities locally and around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand historical aspects of the uniqueness and commonality of individuals and groups, including Maine Native Americans, by describing traditions of Maine Native Americans and various historical and recent immigrant groups and traditions common to all.
description_plain: Your child can describe traditions of Maine Native Americans and other groups, and find things that all people share.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand historical aspects of the uniqueness and commonality of individuals and groups, including Maine Native Americans by explaining how individuals and families share both common and unique aspects of culture, values, and beliefs through stories, traditions, religion, celebrations, or the arts. Students organize findings at a developmentally appropriate manner and share gathered information using oral and visual examples.
description_plain: Your child can explain how families share culture and beliefs through stories and traditions and share what they learn by talking or showing pictures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of history as well as the key foundation of ideas by identifying past, present, and future in stories, pictures, poems, songs, and video.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between past, present, and future using stories, pictures, songs, or videos.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes using primary and secondary sources from history to develop historical perspective and understand issues of continuity and change in the community, Maine, the United States, and world.
description_plain: Your child can use old and new sources to understand history and how things have changed or stayed the same in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the influence of geography on communities by identifying the impacts of geographic features on communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain how land and places affect communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature and basic ideas of geography by gathering information about their immediate neighborhood and community, including maps, photographs, charts and graphs, and then create visual representations of their findings.
description_plain: Your child can gather information about their neighborhood using maps and pictures and make simple drawings to show what they find.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes from geography to understand issues involving people, places, and environments in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child can use geography ideas to understand how people, places, and environments connect in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the influence of economics on individuals and groups in the United States and the World, including Maine Native Americans by identifying how individuals, families, and communities are influenced by economic factors.
description_plain: Your child knows how families and communities are affected by money and work.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of economics as well as key foundational ideas by explaining and making decisions about how to use scarce resources to meet their needs and wants.
description_plain: Your child can explain and decide how to use limited resources to get what they need and want.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of personal finance as well as key foundational ideas by describing how spending, saving, and sharing are ways to use money.
description_plain: Your child knows that spending, saving, and sharing are ways to use money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw from concepts and processes in personal finance to understand issues of money management, saving, investing, credit, and debt; students draw from concepts and processes in economics to understand issues of production, distribution, consumption in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child understands how to manage money, save, invest, use credit, and handle debt, and also how goods and services are made and shared in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand Maine Native Americans by explaining their traditions and customs.
description_plain: Your child can explain the traditions and customs of Maine Native Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the concepts of rights, duties, responsibilities, and participation by explaining the purpose of school/classroom rules and state laws encountered in daily experiences to promote the common good and the peaceful resolution of conflict.
description_plain: Your child understands that rules and laws help keep everyone safe and peaceful at school and in the community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand key ideas and processes that characterize democratic government in the community and the United States by recognizing symbols, monuments, celebrations, and leaders of State government.
description_plain: Your child can recognize important symbols, celebrations, and leaders in state government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts from civics and government to understand political systems, power, authority, governance, civic ideals and practices, and the role of citizens in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child understands how governments work, the role of citizens, and how power and rules affect communities locally and around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand historical aspects of the uniqueness and commonality of individuals and groups, including Maine Native Americans, by describing traditions of Maine Native Americans and various historical and recent immigrant groups and traditions common to all.
description_plain: Your child can describe traditions of Maine Native Americans and other groups and find what they have in common.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of history as well as the key foundation of ideas by creating a brief historical account about family, the local community, or the nation by locating and collecting information from sources including maps, charts, graphs, artifacts, photographs, or stories of the past.
description_plain: Your child can create a short story about family, community, or country history by finding information from maps, pictures, and stories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of history as well as the key foundation of ideas by following an established procedure to locate sources appropriate to reading level and identifying a few key figures and events from personal history and the history of the community, the state, and the United States, especially those associated with historically-based traditions.
description_plain: Your child can find and use information about important people and events from their own history and their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes using primary and secondary sources from history to develop historical perspective and understand issues of continuity and change in the community, Maine, the United States, and world.
description_plain: Your child can use old and new sources to understand history and how things have changed or stayed the same in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the influence of geography on individuals and groups in Maine, including Maine Native Americans, the United States and the world by identifying the impacts of geographic features on individuals and groups in those communities.
description_plain: Your child can explain how land and places affect people and groups in Maine, the U.S., and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature and basic ideas of geography by using basic maps and globes to identify local and distant places and locations, directions (including N, S, E, and W), and basic physical, environmental, and cultural features.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and globes to find places, directions, and learn about land, environment, and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes from geography to understand issues involving people, places, and environments in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child can use geography ideas to understand how people, places, and environments connect in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the influence of economics on individuals and groups in the United States and the World, including Maine Native Americans by describing the work and contributions of various groups to the economics of the local community in the past and present.
description_plain: Your child can describe how different groups have worked and helped the local community’s economy in the past and now.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of economics as well as key foundational ideas by explaining how people make choices about how to use scarce resources and make individual and collaborative plans to meet their own needs and wants.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people decide to use limited resources and make plans to meet their needs and wants.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of personal finance as well as key foundational ideas by describing how planning for the future is important to managing money.
description_plain: Your child can explain why planning ahead is important for managing money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw from concepts and processes in personal finance to understand issues of money management, saving, investing, credit, and debt; students draw from concepts and processes in economics to understand issues of production, distribution, consumption in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child understands how to manage money, save, invest, use credit, and handle debt, and also how goods and services are made and shared in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the traditions of Maine Native Americans and various cultures by comparing national traditions and customs.
description_plain: Your child can compare traditions and customs of Maine Native Americans with those of other cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the concepts of rights, duties, responsibilities, and participation by explaining the purpose of school/classroom rules and national laws encountered in daily experiences to promote the common good and the peaceful resolution of conflict through selecting, planning, and participating in a civic action or service-learning project based on a classroom or school asset or need, and describing the project's potential civic contribution.
description_plain: Your child can explain why school rules and laws help everyone get along and can plan and take part in a project that helps their school or community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand key ideas and processes that characterize democratic government in the community and the United States by recognizing symbols, monuments, celebrations, and leaders of national government.
description_plain: Your child can recognize important national symbols, monuments, celebrations, and leaders.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand key ideas and processes that characterize democratic government in the community and the United States by describing and providing examples of democratic ideals.
description_plain: Your child can describe and give examples of important democratic ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts from civics and government to understand political systems, power, authority, governance, civic ideals and practices, and the role of citizens in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child understands how governments work, the role of citizens, and how power and rules affect communities locally and around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand historical aspects of unity and diversity in the community, the state, Maine Native American communities, and the United States by identifying research questions, seeking multiple perspectives from varied sources, and describing examples in the history of the United States of diverse and shared values and traditions.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions, look at different points of view, and describe examples of shared and different values and traditions in U.S. history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand various major eras in the history of the community, Maine, and the United States by explaining that history includes the study of past human experience based on available evidence from a variety of primary and secondary sources. Students make real or simulated decisions related to the local community or civic organizations by applying appropriate and relevant social studies knowledge and skills, including research skills, and other relevant information.
description_plain: Your child understands that history is about studying the past using different sources and can make decisions about their community using what they learn.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes using primary and secondary sources from history to develop historical perspective and understand issues of continuity and change in the community, Maine, the United States, and world.
description_plain: Your child can use old and new sources to understand history and how things have changed or stayed the same in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand geographic aspects of unity and diversity in the community and in Maine, including Maine Native American communities by collecting, evaluating, and organizing information about the impacts of geographic features on the daily life of various cultures, including Maine Native Americans and other cultures and communities.
description_plain: Your child can gather and organize information about how geography affects daily life in different cultures, including Maine Native Americans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the geography of the community, Maine, the United States, and various regions of the world by explaining that geography includes the study of Earth's physical features including climate and the distribution of plant, animal, and human life.
description_plain: Your child can explain that geography studies Earth's features like climate, plants, animals, and people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes from geography to understand issues involving people, places, and environments in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child can use geography ideas to understand how people, places, and environments connect in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand economic aspects of unity and diversity in the community, Maine, and regions of the United States and the world, including Maine Native American communities by describing economic similarities and differences within the community, Maine, and the United States.
description_plain: Your child can describe how economies in their community, Maine, and the U.S. are similar and different.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand economics and the basis of the economies of the community, Maine, the United States, and various regions of the world by explaining how scarcity leads to choices about what goods and services are produced and for whom they are produced.
description_plain: Your child can explain how limited resources make people decide what goods and services to make and who gets them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the nature of personal finance as well as key foundational ideas by describing situations in which personal choices are related to the use of money.
description_plain: Your child can describe how personal choices affect how money is used.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw from concepts and processes in personal finance to understand issues of money management, saving, investing, credit, and debt; students draw from concepts and processes in economics to understand issues of production, distribution, consumption in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child understands how to manage money, save, invest, use credit, and handle debt, and also how goods and services are made and shared in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies

description_plain: Your child can find examples of things people share and things that are different among cultures in Maine and the U.S.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the basic rights, duties, responsibilities, and roles of citizens in a democratic republic by providing examples of how people influence government and work for the common good including voting and writing to legislators.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how people influence government and help their community by voting or contacting leaders.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the basic rights, duties, responsibilities, and roles of citizens in a democratic republic by identifying the rights, duties, and responsibilities of citizens within the class, school, or community.
description_plain: Your child can identify the rights and responsibilities people have at school and in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the basic ideals, purposes, principles, structures, and processes of democratic government in Maine and the United States by explaining how leaders are elected and how laws are made and implemented.
description_plain: Your child can explain how leaders are chosen and how laws are made and followed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the basic ideals, purposes, principles, structures, and processes of democratic government in Maine and the United States by explaining that the study of government includes how governments are organized and how citizens participate.
description_plain: Your child understands how governments are organized and how people take part in them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts from civics and government to understand political systems, power, authority, governance, civic ideals and practices, and the role of citizens in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child understands how governments work, the role of citizens, and how power and rules affect communities locally and around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand historical aspects of unity and diversity in the community, the state, Maine Native American communities, and the United States by describing various cultural traditions and contributions of Maine Native Americans and various historical and recent immigrant groups in the community and the state.
description_plain: Your child can describe cultural traditions and contributions of Maine Native Americans and immigrant groups in their community and state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand various major eras in the history of the community, Maine, and the United States by identifying major historical eras, major enduring themes, turning points, events, consequences, persons, and timeframes, in the history of the community, the state, and the United States. Students distinguish between facts and opinions/interpretations in sources.
description_plain: Your child can identify important historical times, events, people, and themes in their community, state, and country, and can make decisions using social studies skills while telling facts from opinions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes using primary and secondary sources from history to develop historical perspective and understand issues of continuity and change in the community, Maine, the United States, and world.
description_plain: Your child can use old and new sources to understand history and how things have changed or stayed the same in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand geographic aspects of unity and diversity in various regions of the United States and the world by describing impacts of geographic features on the daily life of various cultures in the United States and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the geography of the community, Maine, the United States, and various regions of the world by communicating their findings by creating visual representations of the world, showing a basic understanding of the geographic grid, including the equator and prime meridian.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students draw on concepts and processes from geography to understand issues involving people, places, and environments in the community, Maine, the United States, and the world.
description_plain: Your child can use geography ideas to understand how people, places, and environments connect in their community, state, country, and the world.

description_plain: Your child can identify economic activities and influences related to Maine Native Americans and other cultures in the U.S. and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand economics and the basis of the economies of the community, Maine, the United States, and various regions of the world by explaining how scarcity leads to choices about how goods and services are consumed and distributed, and by making a real or simulated decision related to scarcity.
description_plain: Your child can explain how limited resources lead to choices about using goods and services and can make a decision about scarcity.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand the principles and process of personal finance by describing situations in which financial institutions can be used to manage money.

description_plain: Your child understands how to manage money, save, invest, use credit, and handle debt, and also how goods and services are made and shared in their community, state, country, and the world.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Students understand various major eras in the history of the community, Maine, and the United States by tracing and explaining how the history of democratic principles is preserved in historic symbols, monuments, and traditions important in the community, Maine and the United States.
